summary: Use cases are a requirements elicitation technique popularized by Ivar Jacobson in the late 80s and early 90s. The post directs readers to Alistair Cockburn's book as the definitive resource on the topic. Fowler argues that while use cases appear in UML as diagrams, the real value lies in the textual descriptions rather than the visual representations. He warns against over-investing time in use case documentation before beginning development.
key_points: Alistair Cockburn's book is the authoritative resource on use cases and should be read before using the technique
Use case diagrams in UML are of little value compared to the textual descriptions they represent
Use cases should be kept short and readable rather than elaborate
Teams should not spend weeks or months on use case documents before beginning development
money_quotes: Use case diagrams are of little value - the key value of use cases lies in the text which is not standardized in UML.
When you do use cases put your energy into the text.
Use cases are at their best when they are short and readable.
You should not be spending weeks, let alone months, generating use case documents before you begin development.
key_insight: The value of use cases lies not in elaborate UML diagrams but in concise, readable text that can be created quickly before development begins.
